rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF LAMBERT GENZ
rulings.Calendar Matter: Respondents Demurrer To Petition To Return Of Estate Assets Filed By Steven Kumcha
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 4] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Stephen M. Murphy. Before the court is Objectors' Demurrer to Petition to Return of Estate Assets Filed by Steven Kumcha. Two days after filing this Demurrer, Objectors also filed a Response/Answer to Petition to Return of Estate Assets from Qubao Yu, Chui F Yu, Bolin Yu, Zi Yu Deng, Paul Yi Wei Deng, Ka Yu Ma, Jianming Yu, Jianjun Yu; For Financial Elder Abuse; Punitive Damages. That filing argues the Petition must be dismissed for lack of jurisdiction under Probate Code section 856.5 and references the pending demurrer. A demurrer is not waived by filing an answer at the same time (Code Civ. Proc. section 472). A demurrer may be filed within the same amount of time a responding party has to file a complaint, i.e., 30 days after service, unless extended by stipulation or court order. (Code of Civil Proc. section 430.40(a).) Per the Minutes of the October 5, 2023 hearing on Petition to Return of Estate Assets, etc., the court directed any objections to the Petition to be filed by November 29, 2023. The Demurrer was filed on December 5, 2023. Per the declaration of counsel in support of the Reply, counsel electronically submitted the Demurrer for filing on November 28, but the filing was rejected and not successfully filed until December 5, the following Tuesday. Counsel has filed a declaration pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 473(b), attesting the late filing was due to his mistake, inadvertence, and neglect. The court will exercise its discretion to consider this untimely Demurrer, based on the Code of Civil Procedure section 473(b) attestation of attorney error. (See, e.g., Jackson v. Doe (2011) 192 CA4th 742, 750.) The court cautions parties and their counsel to heed the lesson of submitting papers for filing the day before the deadline to do so. Objectors, as the demurring parties, also failed to comply with the meet and confer requirements of Code of Civil Procedure section 430.41. Code of Civil Procedure section 430.41(a) requires meet and confer conferences prior to filing a demurrer to be either in-person or telephonic. The attorneys engaged in an exchange of emails and did not meet either telephonically or in person. The attorney for Objectors determined, and so informed attorney Talbot, "[b]ecause there was no possibility that we could resolve the differences in our positions, I told him that I would not be calling him later unless he had other authorities in support of his arguments that he wanted me to consider." (Decl. of Chu at par. 2, 3, 4, 5; Opp. at Exhs. B, C, D.) The Code requires parties to meet and confer in person or telephonically, not through e-mail, and to do so in good faith. Rather than continuing the hearing on the Demurrer or ordering counsel to engage in compliant meet and confer efforts, the court will rule on the Demurrer now in the interests of judicial economy and avoiding further delay in this matter. (See, also, Code of Civil Proc. section 430.41(a)(4)). The parties and their counsel are instructed to be apprised of the applicable law and to follow statutory procedures henceforth.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF LAMBERT GENZ
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Demurrer To Petition To Return Of Estate Assets Filed By Steven Kumcha (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 4 of 4] was procured by undue influence and/or financial elder abuse of Decedent by Objectors Qubao Yu, Chui F Yu, and Bolin Yu. The Petition does not facially plead either the actual or reasonable date of discovery of the facts giving rise to the alleged causes of action, nor otherwise allege facts regarding when any of the causes of action accrued. The factual allegations presented in the Opposition to Demurrer regarding accrual of the relevant statutes of limitation are either not facially plead in the Petition or are presented more clearly in the Opposition as factual allegations, rather than conclusions of law, than they are in the Petition. Since there is a reasonable possibility that the Petition could be amended to cure the defects now subject to demurrer, leave to amend must be granted (see, Galbiso v. Orosi Pub. Util. Dist. (2010) 182 Cal. App. 4th 652, 674). Each special demurrer brought on statute of limitations grounds under Code of Civ. Pro. section 343 and Welf. & Inst. C. section 15657.7 against each cause of action is SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND. Petitioner is directed to file the amended petition within 20 days of Notice of Entry of this Order. Demurring parties shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF LAMBERT GENZ
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Demurrer To Petition To Return Of Estate Assets Filed By Steven Kumcha (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 4] A demurrer is a pleading used to challenge the legal sufficiency of an opponent's pleading based on defects that appear either on the face of the pleading under attack or from matters outside the pleading that are judicially noticeable. (See Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal. 3d 311, 318; County of Fresno v. Shelton (1998) 66 Cal. App. 4th 996, 1008, 1009.) A demurrer must demonstrate no right to any relief under the cause of action. (See, Augustine v. Trucco (1954) 124 Cal.App.2d 229, 236.) When considering a demurrer, the trial court must accept as true all material facts pleaded in the complaint and those arising by reasonable implication. (Moore v. Conliffe (1994) 7 Cal. 4th 634, 638; Montclair Parkowners Ass'n v. City of Montclair (1999) 76 Cal. App. 4th 784.) This does not apply to allegations expressing mere conclusions of law, or allegations contradicted by the exhibits to the complaint or by matters of which judicial notice may be taken. (Vance v. Villa Park Mobilehome Estates (1995) 36 Cal. App. 4th 698, 709.) Objectors bring a general demurrer to the Petition on the basis of lack of jurisdiction. Objectors cite Prob. Code section 856.5 to argue the probate court lacks jurisdiction over the subject Petition, because claims in the Petition should be brought in a civil action (Prob. Code section 856.5: "The court may not grant a petition under this chapter if the court determines that the matter should be determined by a civil action."). After legislative evolution of California probate law beginning in approximately the middle of the previous century (see, e.g. Estate of Abdallah (1947) 80 CA2d 634, 636-637; Estate of Baglione (1966) 65 C2d 192, 196-197), all title disputes and adverse claims to real property involving the interests of a decedent's estate are now resolvable in a probate proceeding under Probate Code section 850(a)(2). Further, the Probate Code specifically allows this type of petition to "include claims, causes of action, or matters that are normally raised in a civil action to the extent that the matters are related factually to the subject matter of a petition filed under this part." (Prob. Code section 855.) The Petition facially alleges sufficient facts placing Petitioner within the scope of parties allowed to petition under this section of the Probate Code: "(a) The following persons may file a petition requesting that the court make an order under this part: [ ] (2) The personal representative or any interested person in any of the following cases: [ ] (D) Where the decedent died having a claim to real or personal property, title to or possession of which is held by another." (Prob. Code section 850). Petitioner was appointed the Administrator of Decedent's estate by this court on March 23, 2023 and Letters issued on April 18, 2023. The Petition alleges the real property at issue is located in San Francisco. (See, generally, Prob. Code section 7050.) The Petition alleges title to the real property at issue rightfully belongs to and must be restored to the estate. The adverse real property claim is brought on the basis of alleged undue influence and elder abuse claims; these claims are "related factually to the subject matter of" the Petition (see, Prob. Code section 855). This court regularly hears petitions brought under Probate Code section 850 et seq with fact patterns and legal theories similar to the allegations and legal theories presented in the Petition.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF LAMBERT GENZ
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Demurrer To Petition To Return Of Estate Assets Filed By Steven Kumcha (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 4] On the current record, the court does not find the matter should be decided in a civil action pursuant to Prob. Code section 856.5. The general demurrer on the grounds of lack of jurisdiction is OVERRULED. Objectors also bring special demurrers to each cause of action on statute of limitations grounds, specifically Code of Civ. Proc. section 343 and Welf. & Inst. C. section 15657.7. The statute of limitations under Code of Civ. Pro. section 343, for an action other than the recovery of real property, is four years after the cause of action has accrued. The statute of limitations under Welf. & Inst. C. section 15657.7 for financial elder abuse is "within four years after the plaintiff discovers or, through the exercise of reasonable diligence, should have discovered, the facts constituting the financial abuse." There is no statutory time limit on filing a petition pursuant to Probate Code section 850. The claim underlying the petition is subject to the applicable statute of limitations, as if the action had been brought as a civil suit. (See, Parker v. Walker (1992) 5 CA4th 1173, 1186 (decided under predecessor statute).) The gravamen of a petition brought under Probate Code section 850, and thus the applicable statute(s) of limitation, depends on the factual allegations of the claim. For example, where the petition brought under Probate Code Section 850 seeking conveyance or transfer of property claimed to belong to the decedent adequately alleges breach of fiduciary duty or undue influence, the probate court may allow a date-of-discovery rule to be applied in analyzing limitations issues, particularly when the defendant or respondent party maintains custody and control of the claimed property or interests. (See, Estate of Young (App. 4 Dist. 2008) 72 Cal.Rptr.3d 520, 160 Cal.App.4th 62.) The Petition here does not present numbered causes of action or matters titled or demarcated "cause of action." Instead, the Petition presents the following sequence of bolded and uppercase headings under the Petition's "Legal Argument" section: Petition for Return of Estate Assets; Decent Lacked Capacity to Execute the 2017 Deed; The 2017 Deed was the Product of Undue Influence; It is Presumed that Qubao, Chui, and Bolin Unduly Influenced Decedent to Execute the 2017 Deed; Qubao, Chui, and Bolin Committed Financial Elder Abuse; Qubao, Chui, and Bolin Acted in Bad Faith. Under each of these headings are corresponding "facts upon which the claim is based," in compliance with Prob. C. section 850(a)(2) and (b), as well as some conclusory legal statements and block-quotes of statutes. Relevant to the gravamen of the Petition and statute of limitations under Welf. & Inst. C. section 15657.7 or Code of Civil Proc. 343, the Petition facially alleges the 2017 Grant Deed at issue is invalid due to Decedent's lack of capacity at relevant and specifically plead time(s), or, in the alternative, it is invalid because it

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE RHEMER TRUST DATED JULY 1, 2011 AS AMENDED AND RESTATED ON OCTOBER 31, 2013 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #2, Report, Fees
rulings.Rulings: Hearing. Appearance required. This matter will be called with PCN-19-303366. 1) Article 2, Paragraph 3(A), of the subject trust provides in relevant part that: "During the lifetime of the Settlor, the Trustee shall pay to the Settlor, or shall apply for his benefit, the entire net income of the trust estate quarter-annually or at more frequent installments. Should the Settlor for any reason be unable to act in his own behalf, the Trustee is authorized to pay to or apply for the benefit of the Settlor such amounts of the principal of the trust estate, up to the whole thereof, as the Trustee, in the Trustee's sole and absolute discretion, determines to be necessary for the Settlor's health, education, support, maintenance, comfort, and welfare in accordance with his accustomed manner of living." During this trust account period, the trust receipts ($58,438) exceeded disbursements ($18,580), producing substantial net income required to be distributed to the conservatee-settlor by Article 2, Paragraph 3(A)(1), of the trust. However, the trustee made no distribution of such net income to the conservatee-settlor, nor did the trustee apply any part of the net income for the conservatee-settlor. Should the conservator be required to petition for substituted judgment under Probate Code section 2580(b)(11) to amend the conservatee's subject trust to relieve the trustee from her mandatory duty to distribute the entire net income under Article 2, Paragraph 3(A)(1), and authorize the trustee in her discretion to retain all or part of net income? 2) Article 2, Paragraph 5(B), of the subject trust provides in relevant part that: "The Settlor wishes to hereby express his appreciation to whomever ultimately serves as Successor Trustee(s), and thus directs that any Successor Trustee serving under this trust be entitled to reasonable compensation for his, her or its efforts at the rate of One Hundred Dollars ($100) per hour for time incurred administering the trust estate." The trustee requests compensation based on $145 per hour for her services until 06/30/2022 and $185 per hour for her services on or after 07/01/2022. Should the conservator be required to petition for substituted judgment under section 2580(b)(11) to amend the conservatee's subject trust to increase the $100/hour rate set by the conservatee-settlor for the trustee's compensation under Article 2, Paragraph 5(B), and authorize the trustee to charge a higher rate subject to the Court's approval, or should the trustee be required to petition under section 15680(b) to request an order overriding the trustee compensation rate set by the conservatee-settlor and fixing greater compensation? PARTIES APPEARING SHOULD CHECK IN FOR THE HEARING WITH THEIR FULL NAME. Counsel and parties may appear personally or remotely through Zoom or CourtCall. For a video appearance, go to zoom.us, click "join a meeting" and input meeting ID 160 225 4765 and password 514879. For an audio-only appearance through Zoom, call 1-669-254-5252 and key in meeting ID "160 225 4765#", then participant ID "0#", then password "514879#". (Toll rates may apply).) rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF WILLIAM DORSEY STUART JR
rulings.Calendar Matter: Verified Petition To Approve Settlement Agreement And Mutual General Release
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 02/14/2024. The following issues must be addressed: 1) No stipulation or joinder signed by Nikki and Ricardo required by section 4 of the settlement agreement is on file. Note: Section 4 requires them to stipulate to and join in this settlement petition, although their stipulation to and joinder in the amended petition for probate of the 2022 will is on file. 2) Nikki has already dismissed her petition for probate with prejudice. Is the "pending litigation" requirement of CCP section 664.6 satisfied to allow the Court to retain jurisdiction to enforce the settlement? See Sayta v. Chu (2017) 17 Cal.App.5th 960. 3) Section 4 of the settlement agreement appears to require a petition for probate of the 2012 will. In light of the amended petition for probate of the 2022 will, it appears necessary to amend the settlement agreement to clarify the rights and obligations of the parties based on the admission of the 2022 will and revocation of the 2012 will.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE JOHNNY J. AND MARGIE CHERRY TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Deeming Matters Specified In Requests For Admission Set 1 And Set 2 Admitted And Imposing Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 3] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Stephen M. Murphy. Now before the Court is Petitioner Windy Cherry's Motion for an Order Deeming Matters Specified in Requests for Admission Set 1 and Set 2 Admitted and Imposing Monetary Sanctions. Respondents filed a late Opposition to the Motion. There is no showing of prejudice to Petitioner caused by the late filing; a Reply to the Opposition was submitted for filing the day after the late Opposition was submitted for filing. The court may decide to consider late filed papers and resolve the matter on the merits. (Gonzalez v. Santa Clara County Dept. of Social Services (2017) 9 Cal. App. 5th 162, 168.) The court will do so here. Petitioner's Request for Judicial notice of certain court records is GRANTED. On its own motion, the court also takes judicial notice of the April 4, 2022 Order After Hearing entered in case number CUD-21-668280 as a court record, under Evid. C. section 452(d). This Order consolidates case numbers CGC-21-594979 and CUD-21-668280 for discovery and trial purposes and stays both, "pending resolution of probate matter in Alameda." Petitioner had filed a probate action re the Johnny J. and Margie Cherry Trust in Alameda County on September 22, 2021. At the time of the April 4, 2022 Order, that probate matter was still pending in Alameda County. The probate matter in Alameda County was subsequently transferred to this court, on April 28, 2023. There is no showing on the record before the court that the stay on the consolidated cases imposed by the April 4, 2022, pending resolution of the probate matter that is now in this court, has been lifted. A notice of related case has not been filed in the probate matter. Opposition to this motion alleges consolidated case numbers CGC-21-594979 and CUD-21-668280 are related to this probate matter. Hearing on this Motion and the two related motions was originally set for November 9, 2023. Hearings on each of the motions were continued to January 17, 2024, to (1) allow an appeal taken against the April 4, 2022 Order issued in allegedly-related case number CUD-21-668280 to be resolved and remitted; (2) allow Respondents to file a motion to consolidate this probate case number with the already-consolidated civil case numbers CGC-21-594979 (lead case) and CUD-21-668280 in Department 501 of this court. On its own motion, the court takes judicial notice of the following entries in case number CUD-21-668280 as a court record, under Evid. C. section 452(d): Remittitur on the appeal was entered November 29, 2023. Respondents' motion to consolidate was denied on procedural grounds in Department 501 on December 29, 2023. There are currently no motions to consolidate pending in either civil case number. The Opposition to the Motion simultaneously indicates that Respondents will file a motion or motions for protective order and appointment for a discovery referee and requests a continuance of this hearing until after those hearings (Opp. at 7:6-12), and also requests this court, at the instant hearing on this Motion, (1) enter a protective order against all the discovery propounded in this case, (2) appoint Ret. Judge Goldsmith as discovery referee under Code of Civil Procedure section 639, and (3) award sanctions against Petitioner for abuse of discovery (Opp. at 4:6-18; 5:16-6:19). The court rules in these requests as follows:

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE JOHNNY J. AND MARGIE CHERRY TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Compelling Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents Set 1 And Imposing Monetary Sanctions (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 3] The request for a protective order is DENIED without prejudice, as procedurally defective (see, Code of Civ. Proc. section 2030.090). The request for appointment of a discovery referee pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 639 is DENIED without prejudice, as procedurally defective. The request for award of sanctions against Petitioner on the instant motion, on the basis of abuse of discovery is DENIED. The Motion for an Order Compelling Responses to Requests for Production of Documents Set 1 and Imposing Monetary Sanctions is GRANTED, for the reasons discussed below. Respondents are ordered to provide Code-compliant responses without objections to Requests for Production of Documents Set 1, served on May 17, 2023, within ten (10) days of service of Notice of Entry of Order. Per the Notice of Motion, the Motion for an Order Compelling Responses to Requests for Production of Documents Set 1 is brought pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 2031.300(b). Under Code of Civ. Proc. section 2031.300(a), "If a party to whom a demand for inspection, copying, testing, or sampling is directed fails to serve a timely response to it, [] [t]he party to whom the demand for inspection, copying, testing, or sampling is directed waives any objection to the demand, including one based on privilege or on the protection for work product under Chapter 4 (commencing with Section 2018.010)." No separate statement is required. (See CRC, Rule 3.1345(b)) There is no statutory "meet-and-confer" requirement. (Code of Civ. Proc. section 2031.300(b).) The motion to compel response to a demand for inspection must show (1) an inspection demand was properly served on the opposing party, (2) the time to respond has expired, and (3) no response of any kind has been served. (Code of Civ. Proc. section 2031.300.) The Motion shows Respondents were properly served Request for Production of Documents, Set 1 by mail on May 17, 2023. (Decl. of Robinson ISO Motion at par. 2, 3, 4, Exhs. 1, 2.) Petitioner timely served Notice of Waiver of Objections on Respondents on June 24, 2023. Written objections to all Request for Production of Documents, Set 1 were served by mail on June 28, 2023, after the time to respond had expired. (Decl. of Robinson ISO Motion at par. 5, 6, Exhs. 3, 4.) In meet and confer efforts, on June 24, 2023, Petitioner offered a June 30, 2023 deadline for Respondents to serve responses without objections. In further meet and confer efforts, Petitioner revised and served Request for Production of Documents, Set 1 on each Respondent in both their individual capacity and their capacity as trustee, via personal service at Respondents' attorney's office on July 6, 2023. There is no indication before the court showing a response was served to this second service of Request for Production of Documents. No response was given to Petitioner's second Notice of Waiver of Objections or further meet and confer efforts. (Decl. of Robinson ISO Motion at par. 7-10, Exhs. 6-11.) There is no indication before the court that Respondents have produced any of the requested discovery since the Motion was filed.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE JOHNNY J. AND MARGIE CHERRY TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Deeming Matters Specified In Requests For Admission Set 1 And Set 2 Admitted And Imposing Monetary Sanctions (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 3] The responding party waives the right to any objections by failing to serve a timely response (Code of Civil Proc. sections 2033.280, 2033.250). Respondents waived their right to object to Requests for Admission Set 1 and Set 2 by failing to respond within the statutory period. The court further finds the objections Respondents served late in response to Requests for Admission Set 1 are invalid and not Code-compliant. (Code of Civil Proc. sections 2033.230; 2033.210(a) and (b); 2033.220; see e.g. Wooldridge v. Mounts (1962) 199 CA2d 620, 628, objections to the entire set of interrogatories.) The Opposition was not submitted with a supporting declaration. Respondents have not met their burden in opposing the Motion to establish whatever facts are necessary to justify the objections. Petitioner has carried the burden in this Motion. Requests for Admission Set 1 was properly served on the opposing party; the time to respond has expired; and no response of any kind has been served. There is no showing Respondents served any responses to either Requests for Admission Set 1 or Set 2 prior to the hearing. Sanctions are awarded pursuant to Civil Procedure sections 2023.030 and 2033.280. On the record currently before the court, there is no showing that either Respondent acted with substantial justification in failing to respond to the propounded discovery, or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust. Respondents Jimmy Cherry and Delphine Cherry and the law firm of KILLIAN & FANNYAN, Attorneys at Law, APC are jointly and severally ordered to pay Petitioner Windy Cherry a total of $ 1,747.50 in monetary sanctions, within 10 days of service of Notice of Entry of Order. (Sanctions award amount calculated based on Decl. of Robinson ISO Motion at par. 16; Decl. of Robinson ISO Reply at par. 5.) Petitioner Windy Cherry shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.

rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 3] Waiver of Objections or further meet and confer efforts. (Decl. of Robinson ISO Motion at par. 7-10, Exhs. 6-11.) There is no indication before the court that Respondents have produced any of the requested discovery since the Motion was filed. The responding party waives the right to any objections by failing to serve a timely response (Code of Civil Proc. section 2030.290). Respondents waived their right to object to Amended Special Interrogatories Set 1 by failing to respond within the statutory time period. The court further finds the late objections to the Amended Special Interrogatories Set 1 are invalid and not Code-compliant. (Code of Civil Proc. sections 2031.240; see e.g. Wooldridge v. Mounts (1962) 199 CA2d 620, 628, objections to the entire set of interrogatories.) The Opposition was not submitted with a supporting declaration or verification. Respondents have not met their burden in opposing the motion to compel answers to establish whatever facts are necessary to justify the objections. Petitioner has carried the burden in this Motion. Amended Special Interrogatories Set 1 was properly served on the opposing party; the time to respond has expired; and no response of any kind has been served. There is no showing Respondents served any responses to Amended Special Interrogatories Set 1 or Set 2 prior to the hearing. Respondents are ordered to provide Code-compliant responses without objections to Special Interrogatories Set 1, served on May 17, 2023, and Set 2, served on July 6, 2023, within ten (10) days of service of Notice of Entry of Order. Sanctions are awarded pursuant to Civil Procedure sections 2023.010 and 2030.290(b) and (c). On the record currently before the court, there is no showing that either Respondent acted with substantial justification in failing to respond to the propounded discovery, or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust. Respondents Jimmy Cherry and Delphine Cherry and the law firm of KILLIAN & FANNYAN, Attorneys at Law, APC are jointly and severally ordered to pay Petitioner Windy Cherry a total of $ 1,747.50 in monetary sanctions, within 10 days of service of Notice of Entry of Order. (Sanctions award amount calculated based on Decl. of Robinson ISO Motion at par. 16; Decl. of Robinson ISO Reply at par. 5.) Petitioner Windy Cherry shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE JOHNNY J. AND MARGIE CHERRY TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Compelling Responses To Special Interrogatories Set 1 And Set 2 And Imposing Monetary Sanctions (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 3] The request for a protective order is DENIED without prejudice, as procedurally defective (see, Code of Civ. Proc. section 2030.090). The request for appointment of a discovery referee pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 639 is DENIED without prejudice, as procedurally defective. The request for award of sanctions against Petitioner on the instant motion, on the basis of abuse of discovery is DENIED. The Motion for an Order Compelling Responses to Special Interrogatories Set 1 and Set 2 and Imposing Monetary Sanctions is GRANTED, for the reasons discussed below. Respondents are ordered to provide Code-compliant responses without objections to Special Interrogatories Set 1, served on May 17, 2023, and Set 2, served on July 6, 2023, within ten (10) days of service of Notice of Entry of Order. Per the Notice of Motion, the Motion for an Order Compelling Responses to Special Interrogatories Set 1 and Set 2 is brought pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 2030.290(b). Under Code of Civil Procedure section 2030.290(a), "If a party to whom interrogatories are directed fails to serve a timely response, [] [t]he party to whom the interrogatories are directed waives any right to exercise the option to produce writings under Section 2030.230, as well as any objection to the interrogatories, including one based on privilege or on the protection for work product under Chapter 4 (commencing with Section 2018.010)." The motion must show only: (1) a set of interrogatories was properly served on the opposing party; (2) the time to respond has expired; and (3) no response of any kind has been served. (Leach v. Sup. Ct (1980) 111 Cal. App. 3d 902, 905-906.) A motion to compel responses to unanswered interrogatories or inspection demands is not subject to a statutory time limit, and the propounding party does not have to demonstrate either good cause or that it satisfied a meet and confer requirement. (Sinaiko Healthcare Consulting, Inc. v. Pacific Healthcare Consultants (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 390; Code of Civ. Proc. section 2030.290; Leach v. Sup. Ct (1980) 111 Cal. App. 3d 902, 905-906.) No separate statement is required as part of the motion. (See CRC, Rule 3.1345(b)). The record before the court shows Respondents were properly served Amended Special Interrogatories Set 1 by mail on May 17, 2023. (Decl. of Robinson ISO Motion at par. 2, 3, 4, Exhs. 1, 2.) Petitioner timely served Notice of Waiver of Objections on Respondents on June 24, 2023. Written objections to all Amended Special Interrogatories Set 1 were served by mail on June 28, 2023, after the time to respond had expired (Decl. of Robinson ISO Motion at par. 5, 6, Exhs. 3, 4; see Code of Civil Proc. sections 2030.210, 2030.260, 2030.270). In meet and confer efforts, on June 24, 2023, Petitioner offered a June 30, 2023 deadline for Respondents to serve responses without objections. In further meet and confer efforts, Petitioner revised and served Amended Special Interrogatories Set 1 on each Respondent in both their individual capacity and their capacity as trustee, via personal service at Respondents' attorney's office on July 6, 2023. There is no indication before the court showing a response was served to this second set of special interrogatories; no response was given to Petitioner's second Notice of

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE JOHNNY J. AND MARGIE CHERRY TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Deeming Matters Specified In Requests For Admission Set 1 And Set 2 Admitted And Imposing Monetary Sanctions (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 3] The request for a protective order is DENIED without prejudice, as procedurally defective (see, Code of Civ. Proc. section 2030.090). The request for appointment of a discovery referee pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 639 is DENIED without prejudice, as procedurally defective. The request for award of sanctions against Petitioner on the instant motion, on the basis of abuse of discovery is DENIED. Motion for an Order Deeming Matters Specified in Requests for Admission Set 1 and Set 2 Admitted and Imposing Monetary Sanctions is GRANTED, for the reasons discussed below. Per the Notice of Motion, the Motion for an Order Deeming Matters Specified in Requests for Admission Set 1 and Set 2 Admitted is brought pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 2033.280(b). Under Code of Civil Procedure section 2033.280(a), "If a party to whom requests for admission [RFAs] are directed fails to serve a timely response, [] [t]he party to whom the requests for admission are directed waives any objection to the requests, including one based on privilege or on the protection for work product under Chapter 4 (commencing with Section 2018.010)." The motion to deem admissions admitted must show (1) a set of requests for admission was properly served on the opposing party; (2) the time to respond has expired; and (3) no response of any kind has been served. (Code of Civil Procedure sections 2033.250, 2033.280.) No separate statement is required. (CRC 3.1345(b).) The moving party is not required to have met and conferred (Code of Civ. Proc. section 2033.280(a); St. Mary v. Sup. Ct (2014) 223 Cal. App. 4th 762, 777-778.) The court must order the RFAs deemed admitted if the moving party has made the required showing. (Code of Civ. Proc. sections 2033.220, 2033.280(c); St. Mary v. Sup. Ct (2014) 223 Cal. App. 4th 762, 778.) The Motion shows Respondents were properly served Amended Requests for Admission Set 1 by mail on May 17, 2023. (Decl. of Robinson ISO Motion at par. 2, 3, 4, Exhs. 1, 2.) Petitioner timely served Notice of Waiver of Objections on Respondents on June 24, 2023. Written objections to all Requests for Admission Set 1 were served by mail on June 28, 2023, after the time to respond had expired. (Decl. of Robinson ISO Motion at par. 5, 6, Exhs. 3, 4.) In meet and confer efforts, on June 24, 2023, Petitioner offered a June 30, 2023 deadline for Respondents to serve responses without objections. In further meet and confer efforts, Petitioner revised and served Requests for Admission Set 1 on each Respondent in both their individual capacity and their capacity as trustee, via personal service at Respondents' attorney's office on July 6, 2023. There is no indication before the court showing any response was served to this second service of Requests for Admission. No response was given to Petitioner's second Notice of Waiver of Objections or further meet and confer efforts. (Decl. of Robinson ISO Motion at par. 7-10, Exhs. 6-11.) There is no indication before the court that Respondents have produced any of the requested discovery since the Motion was filed.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF ROBERT BENJAMIN CROSBY
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR LETTERS OF ADMINISTRATION
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 02/14/2024, as the following issues (e-mailed to counsel on 11/27/2023) remain unaddressed: 1) No proposed order has been received or lodged. 2) No DE-147 form is on file. 3) No DE-147S form is on file. This form must be filed confidentially and conventionally with a pleading cover sheet attached (cannot be e-filed or attached to any e-filed document). 4) No DE-121 form is on file. A copy of the remote appearance information required by LRSF 14.1(C)(6) must be attached to and served with the form at least 15 days before the hearing. 5) No proof of publication is on file. 6) Item 3e(3) (rather than 3e(2)) of the petition must be checked, if the decedent died intestate. 7) Item 3g(2)(b) must be checked. 8) Item 5a(3) or (4) must be checked. 9) Item 8: i) The petitioner must be listed. ii) The names and dates of death of all deceased children of the decedent must be listed. iii) The decedent's child who was a parent of each grandchild listed at item 8 must be identified.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF KERN YEE WONG
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR PROBATE OF WILL AND FOR LETTERS TESTAMENTARY
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 2/14/2024. The following issues are to be cleared two weeks prior. (1) Need to re-notice and re-publish to mirror that Petition offers a will to be admitted. Notice #4 is silent; thus, the publication does not indicate the information. Also, notice and publication to indicate the Amended Petition being filed. (2) Need Confidential Statement of Birth Date and Driver's License Number [JC Form DE-147S] to be filed. See the Local Rule of SF 14.18.E. The document was attached to a declaration; however, it must be filed conventionally with a proper cover sheet. (3) Petition #2.b to set forth the proposed personal representative as Administrator with Will Annexed, not Executor. Prob. Code sec. 8440. It appears the Amended Petition revised the caption only. (4) Petition #8 to identify the successor(s)-in-interest of Bryan. If any, the successor-in-interest must be noticed. Prob. Code sec. 8110(b). Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10.B.
